name: dam_flood_gate_farmland_release
premise: Harlan County faces an overnight storm forecast that could overtop Cedar Fork Dam unless operators release floodwater through agricultural gates that protect downstream towns. Dam safety chief Ren Park controls the release schedule. Farmer coalition lead Dana Whitfield shows soil tests proving that a full release will destroy winter wheat for two hundred contract farmworkers and contaminate irrigation ponds used by a rural clinic. Partial release lowers town risk but leaves a narrow probability of dam stress that could fail entirely. The governor's office has not returned calls; the clock is ninety minutes to crest. National Weather Service models disagree on rainfall intensity by twelve percent.

agents.name: Ren Park
agents.identity: I am the dam safety chief accountable to downstream municipalities and state dam regulators for structural integrity during extreme weather.
agents.goal: I want to minimize catastrophic breach risk while acknowledging that my release curve directly destroys livelihoods upstream of the gates.

agents.name: Dana Whitfield
agents.identity: I am a farmer coalition representative who documents soil erosion, crop loss, and labor impacts when flood operations sacrifice agricultural buffers.
agents.goal: I want releases staged so families and migrant workers are not wiped out when alternative timing might still protect towns.

choices: I will authorize the full protective release immediately to prioritize downstream population centers, accepting documented crop and pond damage.
I will stage a slower release curve that keeps marginal dam stress higher for two more radar cycles, accepting increased breach probability if the storm intensifies.
forced_choice.call_to_action: County emergency radio is demanding a decision before the river crest window closes. Ren Park must choose between maximum downstream protection and a staged release that keeps more farmland intact. What does Ren Park do?
